aliases: Double Fish Burger with Spicy Tomato Sauce

english: Double Fish Burger with Spicy Tomato Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a double fish burger with spicy tomato sauce. FOODON_00002473 (fish sandwich) is the closest defensible parent class. The double-patty and spicy tomato sauce components are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fish burger) is the closest defensible match. The double-patty configuration and spicy tomato sauce are not distinguished at this level of the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to a spicy tomato double fish burger. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for composite fast-food sandwich items of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, representing a complete assembled double fish burger with bun, two battered fish patties, spicy tomato sauce, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Tomato Double Fish Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Tomato Double Fish Burger (cr_id G00010111). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported in the HPB record are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread and refined-flour burger bun analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a spicy tomato double fish burger. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a refined white-flour burger bun, which has a published GI of approximately 70 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The battered fish coating contributes additional refined carbohydrate, supporting a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 59.14 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 41.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 487 mg/100 g with low potassium-to-sodium ratio (197 mg K vs 487 mg Na per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this item; a single 200 g serving delivers approximately 974 mg sodium (over 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and the low potassium content (394 mg per serving) provides insufficient offset to mitigate the pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100 g) from white-flour bun and battered coating, with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) and elevated sugar (5.95 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this item; the high-GI refined bun and battered fish coating produce a glycemic load of approximately 41 per serving, and the low fibre content (3.6 g per 200 g serving) provides minimal attenuation of postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation, high sodium (487 mg/100 g), cholesterol (29 mg/100 g), and likely trans fat from commercial deep-frying; ultra-processed classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item; the combination of deep-fried battered fish patties, high sodium, cholesterol, and ultra-processed ingredients is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns, and regular consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); tomato sauce contains nightshade solanine; likely trans fat present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item; double deep-fried fish patties generate a high AGE load, trans fat is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier, and tomato-based nightshade sauce may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Spicy Tomato Double Fish Burger is a Singapore fast-food sandwich consisting of two battered, deep-fried white fish fillets served in a refined white-flour burger bun with spicy tomato sauce, lettuce, and mayonnaise. The dish is classified as ultra-processed and is a Western-style fast-food adaptation popular in Singapore. Each 100 g provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 5.95 g sugar and only 1.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 487 mg/100 g, reflecting seasoned batter, spicy tomato sauce, and mayonnaise contributions. The texture is crispy on the outside from the battered coating and soft inside from the fish fillet, with a tangy, mildly spicy flavour profile from the tomato-based sauce. Clinically, this item is of high concern for hypertension (high sodium, poor potassium offset), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI refined carbohydrates, low fibre, elevated sugar, glycemic load ~41 per 200 g serving), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried, ultra-processed, high sodium, cholesterol present). The deep-frying process destroys omega-3 fatty acids naturally present in fish and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this item unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Iron content is notable at 2.87 mg/100 g, and calcium is 116 mg/100 g, likely from the bun and fish. This item should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by healthy adults, and should be avoided by individuals managing hypertension, diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Core macro and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010111) at the source-assigned confidence of 0.95. The food name, description, and food group are canonical and consistent with the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the refined white-flour bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which is the standard defensible approach when no direct GI measurement exists for a composite fast-food item.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item (G00010111) and are not available in any other approved source for this specific composite fast-food product as of 2026-04-16. 2. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced trans_fat at 0.1 g/100 g; however, this value is not present in the HPB per_100g record and has not been independently confirmed in an approved source. Trans fat is therefore set to -1 (not reported) rather than 0.1, and the rheumatoid arthritis concern is retained on the basis of deep-frying AGE generation and nightshade content alone. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on refined white-flour burger bun (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite item exists in any approved source. 4. Halal status is assumed based on the Singapore fast-food context and fish-based composition; formal halal certification should be verified with the specific vendor. 5. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a standard double fish burger; actual weight may vary by vendor.
